# Overview [CURRENT] — package: overview — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

Every agent call — whether orchestrator, discovery, planning, ticket, data-layer operation, or controller conversation — flows through the same execution pipeline. The pipeline is structured as a **four-stage decorator chain** (request → prompt → tool → result) orchestrated by `AgentExecutor`, with `BlackboardHistory` providing per-session state persistence and `DecorateRequestResults` coordinating the decorator collections. Understanding this pipeline is essential because every observable behavior (events, graph node creation, worktree attachment, prompt assembly, error recovery) is implemented as a decorator, not inline in agent code.

# The Execution Pipeline [CURRENT] — package: execution-pipeline — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 15 files tracked (package: execution-pipeline) — see `$.sections["The Execution Pipeline"]` in .metadata/HEAD

When an Embabel `@Agent` route (e.g., `DiscoveryRoute`, `OrchestratorRoute`) needs to call the LLM, it calls `AgentExecutor.run()`. This method executes a strict 9-step process:

```
Agent Route (@Agent @Action)
 │
 ▼
AgentExecutor.run(AgentExecutorArgs)
 │
 ├─ 1. REQUEST DECORATION ─────── DecorateRequestResults.decorateRequest()
 │      sorted by order():          or .decorateResultsRequest() for ResultsRequest types
 │      EmitActionStartedRequestDecorator (MIN_VALUE — runs first, emits ActionStartedEvent)
 │      RegisterBlackboardHistoryInputRequestDecorator (records input to history)
 │      SetGoalRequestDecorator (writes goal onto blackboard)
 │      WorktreeContextRequestDecorator (attaches worktree paths)
 │      PropagateActionRequestDecorator (runs pre-action data-layer propagators)
 │      StartWorkflowRequestDecorator (10000 — creates/updates graph nodes via WorkflowGraphService)
 │
 ├─ 2. TEMPLATE MODEL PRODUCTION ─ TemplateModelProducer lambda from the route
 │
 ├─ 3. PROMPT CONTEXT BUILD ────── PromptContextFactory.build() + PromptContextDecorators
 │      DefaultPromptContextDecorator, FilterPropertiesDecorator
 │      → produces PromptContext (agent type, template, chat ID, error state)
 │
 ├─ 4. TOOL CONTEXT BUILD ──────── ToolContextDecorators
 │      AddAcpTools, AddTopologyTools, AddSkillToolContextDecorator,
 │      AddMemoryToolCallDecorator, AddIntellij / RemoveIntellij
 │
 ├─ 5. EXECUTOR START EVENT ────── publishes AgentExecutorStartEvent (unique nodeId per attempt)
 │
 ├─ 6. ERROR TEMPLATE RESOLUTION ─ resolveTemplate() checks BlackboardHistory.errorType()
 │      if last error was CompactionError → use compaction template
 │      if ParseError → use parse error template, etc.
 │      ErrorTemplates are per-action metadata on AgentActionMetadata
 │
 ├─ 7. LLM CALL ───────────────── LlmRunner.runWithTemplate()
 │      LlmCallDecorators run first (PromptHealthCheckLlmCallDecorator, ArtifactEmissionLlmCallDecorator)
 │      → assembles prompt via PromptContributorService
 │      → calls ACP chat model
 │
 ├─ 8. EXECUTOR COMPLETE EVENT ─── publishes AgentExecutorCompleteEvent (same nodeId links to start)
 │
 └─ 9. RESULT DECORATION ──────── DecorateRequestResults.decorateRouting()
        WorkflowGraphResultDecorator (updates graph nodes: pending/complete via WorkflowGraphService)
        PropagateActionResultDecorator (runs post-action propagators)
        EmitActionCompletedResultDecorator (MAX_VALUE — runs last, emits ActionCompletedEvent)
```

The `order()` on each decorator controls sequencing. `EmitActionStarted` runs at `MIN_VALUE` (first); `StartWorkflow` at `10000` (after enrichment); `EmitActionCompleted` at `MAX_VALUE` (last, with the fully decorated result).

# Request/Result Decorator Symmetry [CURRENT] — package: request-result-decorator-symmetry — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 41 files tracked (package: request-result-decorator-symmetry) — see `$.sections["Request/Result Decorator Symmetry"]` in .metadata/HEAD

The request and result sides are mirror images that jointly manage the graph node lifecycle:

**Request side** (`StartWorkflowRequestDecorator`): Pattern-matches on the request type (via exhaustive `switch`) and calls the corresponding `WorkflowGraphService.start*()` method. For example, `DiscoveryOrchestratorRequest` → `startDiscoveryOrchestrator()` → creates/reuses the node → marks it RUNNING. For `InterruptRequest` subtypes, it calls `handleAgentInterrupt()` instead.

**Result side** (`WorkflowGraphResultDecorator`): Pattern-matches on the routing type and calls the corresponding `WorkflowGraphService.pending*()` or `complete*()` method. Routing types that carry an `interruptRequest` field get interrupt handling. Agent results (Discovery/Planning/TicketAgentRouting) resolve the running node by looking up the original request's `contextId` from the `DecoratorContext`.

This pairing means every agent call goes through: **request decorator creates/starts node → LLM runs → result decorator completes/pends node**. The graph is always consistent because both sides are in the same pipeline.

# The Fan-Out Dispatch Process [CURRENT] — package: fan-out-dispatch — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 14 files tracked (package: fan-out-dispatch) — see `$.sections["The Fan-Out Dispatch Process"]` in .metadata/HEAD

For dispatched agents (discovery/planning/ticket), `AgentExecutor` has dedicated methods (`runDiscoveryDispatch`, `runPlanningDispatch`, `runTicketDispatch`) that implement the fan-out/fan-in pattern:

1. Decorate the container request (e.g., `DiscoveryAgentRequests`)
2. Iterate over individual requests, running each as a **subprocess** via `ActionContext.asSubProcess()` — this triggers the Embabel framework to run the dispatch sub-agent (`WORKFLOW_DISCOVERY_DISPATCH_SUBAGENT`, etc.)
3. If any subprocess returns an interrupt, abort and propagate the interrupt routing
4. Collect all results into a `ResultsRequest` (e.g., `DiscoveryAgentResults`)
5. Run the full `AgentExecutor.run()` pipeline with the collected results as input — this is the **consolidation LLM call** that produces the dispatch routing

The key insight: each subprocess is itself a full Embabel agent execution that goes through its own decorator pipeline (including graph node creation for the leaf `DiscoveryNode`/`PlanningNode`/`TicketNode`). The consolidation step is a separate LLM call that sees all results pretty-printed via `AgentPretty`.

**Python view-agent-exec bridge** — Discovery and planning dispatch subagents can invoke the Python `view-agent-exec` CLI via ACP to query repository views. The dispatch agent's skill tool calls `view-agent query` with `--command`/`--args`/`--artifact-key`. The parent agent's `ArtifactKey` is threaded through so view query results trace back to the originating dispatch node. Sandbox args from `application.yml` are passed through to the ACP subprocess spawned by the Python runtime (`ACPLLMProvider`), ensuring the same isolation guarantees as Java-side agent execution. The Python `AcpRuntime` can be shared across parallel fan-out queries to reuse event bus and session infrastructure.

# BlackboardHistory: Session State and Loop Detection [CURRENT] — package: blackboard-history — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

`BlackboardHistory` is the per-session memory that ties the decorator pipeline together. It lives on the Embabel `Blackboard` (accessed via `OperationContext`) and serves three critical functions:

1. **Action history**: Every request and result passes through `RegisterBlackboardHistoryInputRequestDecorator`, which calls `history.addEntry()`. This creates a chronological log of `Entry` records (action name, input object, timestamp, input type). The history is how decorators like `StartWorkflowRequestDecorator` can find the "last workflow request" — it walks the entries backwards, skipping interrupt/communication/utility requests.

2. **Workflow graph state**: `WorkflowGraphState` is stored on the history and tracks the current node IDs for each phase role (orchestrator, dispatch, collector for discovery/planning/tickets). `WorkflowGraphService` reads and writes this state via `resolveState()` / `updateState()`, which access the blackboard through the `OperationContext`.

3. **Error tracking and loop detection**: Errors are recorded as `ErrorDescriptor` entries. The history tracks `AgentExecutorStartEvent` / `AgentExecutorCompleteEvent` pairs to identify retry sequences — if a start has no matching complete, the agent is being retried. `errorCountForRetrySequence()` counts errors within the current retry chain (not across completed executions). Loop detection uses `detectLoop()` with a configurable threshold (default 3) to prevent degenerate agent loops.

# Controller Conversation Pipeline [CURRENT] — package: controller-conversation-pipeline — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 6 files tracked (package: controller-conversation-pipeline) — see `$.sections["Controller Conversation Pipeline"]` in .metadata/HEAD

When an agent calls the controller (via `AgentTopologyTools.callController`), a parallel pipeline runs:

1. `AgentExecutor.controllerExecution()`: Runs the full decoration chain (request → prompt → prompt contributors) but **instead of calling the LLM**, publishes an interrupt and blocks via `InterruptService.publishAndAwaitControllerInterrupt()`. The fully rendered prompt becomes the interrupt content.

2. `AgentExecutor.controllerResponseExecution()`: When the controller responds, this runs the decoration chain for `ControllerToAgentRequest`, renders the response through prompt contributors, then resolves the original interrupt via `PermissionGate.resolveInterrupt()`.

The key hierarchy: `interruptId` = `callingKey.createChild()` (set when the agent initiated the call). The controller response creates a child contextId under the interrupt, and the `chatId` points to the agent's session (so the response goes to the right LLM conversation).

# Error Recovery and Retry [CURRENT] — package: error-recovery-retry — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 8 files tracked (package: error-recovery-retry) — see `$.sections["Error Recovery and Retry"]` in .metadata/HEAD

When the LLM fails (parse error, timeout, compaction, null result), the Embabel framework retries via `ActionRetryListener`. On retry:

1. `ErrorDescriptor` is recorded on the blackboard history (by the retry infrastructure)
2. Next `AgentExecutor.run()` call detects the error via `history.errorType(sessionKey)` — scoped to the current retry sequence
3. `resolveTemplate()` replaces the normal Jinja template with an error-specific template from `ErrorTemplates` (e.g., `compactionFirstTemplate`, `parseErrorTemplate`)
4. The error template model contains `errorDetail` from the exception message
5. Compaction errors have two tiers: `compactionFirstTemplate` (first occurrence) and `compactionMultipleTemplate` (repeated compaction failures)

The retry sequence tracking is sophisticated: `findFirstUnmatchedStartIndex(sessionKey)` walks the history to find the chain of `AgentExecutorStartEvent` entries without matching `AgentExecutorCompleteEvent` entries (matched by unique `nodeId`, not `requestContextId` which is shared across retries).

**ACP-layer retry context** — `AcpRetryEventListener` maintains a parallel per-session retry state (`AcpSessionRetryContext`) at the ACP transport layer. It subscribes to lifecycle events (`ChatSessionCreatedEvent`, `AgentExecutorStartEvent/CompleteEvent`, `CompactionEvent`, `NullResultEvent`, `IncompleteJsonEvent`, `UnparsedToolCallEvent`, `TimeoutEvent`) and tracks `ErrorCategory` + `CompactionStatus` per session. `AcpChatModel` queries this context to decide whether the current call is a retry and which error category triggered it — this allows the ACP layer to adjust its behavior (e.g., session management, timeout tuning) independently of the Embabel-level retry template selection. The two layers are complementary: Embabel retry controls *what prompt* the agent sees; ACP retry context controls *how the transport* behaves.

# AgentInterfaces and Metadata Registry [CURRENT] — package: agent-interfaces-metadata — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 3 files tracked (package: agent-interfaces-metadata) — see `$.sections["AgentInterfaces and Metadata Registry"]` in .metadata/HEAD

`AgentInterfaces.MultiAgentIdeMetadata.AgentMetadata` is a static registry of all agent actions. Each `AgentActionMetadata<T, U, V>` record captures: agent name, action name, method name, agent type, template path, error templates, and the request/routing/result type parameters. This is how the system knows which template to use, which error templates apply, and how to type-check the decorator pipeline for any given action. The `findByAction()` method looks up metadata at runtime (used by `runDirect()` for callers that already have their own contexts).

# Communication Topology [CURRENT] — package: communication-topology — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

The topology system controls which agents can communicate during execution. CommunicationTopologyConfig (YAML-driven) defines allowed communication pairs per AgentType, maxCallChainDepth, and messageBudget. CommunicationTopologyProvider wraps the config for runtime queries. AgentCommunicationService enforces the topology: listAvailableAgents filters by topology-permitted, session-open, and not-self; validateCall rejects disallowed pairs. The reachableVia field enables indirect routing — if agent A cannot call B directly, it reports which intermediaries can reach B. AgentTopologyTools (list_agents, call_agent, call_controller) are the MCP tools agents use to communicate, each annotated with @SetFromHeader(MCP_SESSION_HEADER) for session identity.

**Agent checklists** — The controller skill maintains per-agent-type checklists in `conversational-topology/` that define the review contract for each phase gate. `checklist.md` is the general phase-gate review checklist (applies to all agents). Agent-specific checklists: `checklist-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-discovery-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-discovery-dispatch.md`, `checklist-discovery-agent.md`, `checklist-discovery-collector.md`, `checklist-planning-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-planning-dispatch.md`, `checklist-planning-agent.md`, `checklist-planning-collector.md`, `checklist-ticket-orchestrator.md`, `checklist-ticket-dispatch.md`, `checklist-ticket-agent.md`, `checklist-ticket-collector.md`, `checklist-orchestrator-collector.md`. Always load the general checklist AND the agent-specific checklist before approving any call_controller conversation.

# Blackboard Planner [CURRENT] — package: blackboard-planner — rendered: 2026-04-14T20:16:23Z

References: 2 files tracked (package: blackboard-planner) — see `$.sections["Blackboard Planner"]` in .metadata/HEAD

`BlackboardRoutingPlanner` replaces the default Embabel GOAP planner with a fast type-matching router. Instead of evaluating preconditions across all actions, it reads the last blackboard entry's type and matches it against each action's input bindings:

1. `BlackboardRoutingPlannerFactory.create()` registers the planner with `DefaultAgentPlatform`
2. On each routing step, `planToGoal()` reads the current `BlackboardWorldState`
3. If the last entry is an `AgentModels.Routing` (a `SomeOf` record), it extracts the first non-null record component as the actual routed request
4. Matches the extracted type against each action's `inputBindings` via `satisfiesType()`
5. Returns a single-action `ConditionPlan` — no multi-step planning

This is why every `@Action` method in the workflow agents has a `when` condition that checks a specific routing field — the planner dispatches based on type matching, not condition evaluation.
